response:
# Decision: v1 geocoding sunset

**Option chosen: A (firm sunset), with the date and outreach calibrated to what the complaints actually show — not the memo's "e.g. six months" placeholder.**

## Terms
- **Sunset date:** 2027-04-14 (9 months from today, 2026-07-14) — not 6 months. NorthRoute's ask isn't a config change, it's a parser rewrite plus "a full QA pass on maps our dispatchers use live" (complaints.md). A live-dispatcher-facing rewrite-and-QA cycle is a real project; 6 months was never derived from that timeline, it was a round number in the memo. 9 months is still a guess, but it's a guess anchored to the one concrete scope description we have, not to convenience.
- **Usage threshold:** dropped as a *timing* input. 0.4% justifies committing to termination at all (this is genuinely a thin, unowned tail, so killing it is safe to commit to) — but it does not set the date, because the complaints directly show traffic share doesn't track migration cost. C is rejected on this basis.
- **Notice schedule:** immediate, multi-channel notice today to every account with v1 traffic in the last full month, not just the three who wrote in — email + in-dashboard banner + direct outreach to top-volume v1 accounts. Reminders at T-6mo, T-3mo, T-30d, T-7d. This exists specifically because the three complainants are the engaged fraction of a "long, thin tail: no single large customer, mostly old integrations nobody ever updated" (MEMO.md) — the accounts most likely to miss a deadline are the ones not currently emailing anyone.
- **Hands-on help:** dedicated engineering support offered to NorthRoute, Tavo, and Ferrymark. Extensions beyond 2027-04-14 are available case-by-case only to accounts that have demonstrably started migrating and can show a specific blocker — not as a blanket delay to any account that hasn't started, since "no bandwidth this year" (Tavo) and "nobody has time right now" (Ferrymark) are exactly the kind of open-ended objections that never resolve on their own and would collapse this into option B.
- **B is rejected outright:** the memo's own framing is that every bugfix is already written and tested twice, and "until complaints stop" has no exit condition — vivid complaints about load-bearing systems don't stop on their own, so B guarantees the double-maintenance cost keeps compounding indefinitely.

## Load-bearing assumption
**Per-customer migration cost, for the realistic population of v1 integrations, fits inside a 9-month runway when paired with hands-on help for accounts that engage.** If false — if work like NorthRoute's shape rewrite plus live-dispatcher QA systematically runs longer than 9 months even with direct engineering support — this decision reverses, because the firm date stops being a deadline and becomes a scheduled breakage event.

## Failure class accepted
Some fraction of the unengaged tail — old integrations with no current owner, notices landing in inboxes nobody watches — will not migrate in time and will break at cutover, discovered after the fact rather than pre-empted. This is accepted as *bounded* breakage: 0.4% of traffic, no single large customer, multi-channel outreach specifically built to shrink that fraction. It's accepted in preference to the alternative failure class guaranteed by B: unresolved, compounding double-maintenance cost with no forcing function, and the team "circling back" on this exact question again in another six months.

premortem.response:
Read all three files. Premortem below — assuming whatever call got made six months ago, here's what plausibly took it down.

**1. The runway was set without the one number that matters, and it was wrong for the accounts it targeted.**
The memo itself names this gap: "I do not have a good estimate of per-customer migration cost... I am deciding partly in the dark on the one input that matters most" (MEMO.md). NorthRoute's email isn't vague hand-wringing — they describe a concrete scope: rewriting a parsing layer around v1's flat `lat`/`lng`/`label` shape versus v2's nested `location` object, plus "a full QA pass on maps our dispatchers use live" (complaints.md). If the chosen date/threshold was picked as a round number (six months, or 60 days under option C) rather than derived from that actual rewrite-and-QA timeline, the failure mode is mechanical: the deadline hits before the migration is done, and it breaks a live dispatcher-facing system mid-flight — not because the customer ignored notices, but because the runway was never calibrated to the work.

**2. The three loud accounts weren't the real risk population — the silent tail was.**
v1 is "a long, thin tail: no single large customer, mostly old integrations nobody ever updated" (MEMO.md) — 41k requests across presumably many more than three integrations. The three who wrote in are the ones with enough engagement to complain, and even they say they can't act: Tavo has "no engineering bandwidth this year" and Ferrymark says "nobody here has time to touch it right now" (complaints.md). If the visible, motivated accounts are already this stretched, the invisible rest of that tail — old integrations with no current owner, notices landing in unmonitored inboxes — is structurally more likely to do nothing until the date arrives. A staged notice schedule addressed to known contacts doesn't reach integrations nobody is watching; the failure surfaces as unattributed breakage after cutover, discovered downstream rather than pre-empted.

**3. The decision didn't actually resolve the thing it was meant to resolve.**
The stated problem is that v1/v2 have "drifted far enough apart that every geocoding bugfix now has to be written and tested twice" (MEMO.md), and the team has "circled back" on this before. Option B (indefinite, until complaints stop) has no real exit condition — "until complaints stop" isn't a metric, and vivid, specific complaints (a live dispatcher workflow, a load-bearing nightly job) don't stop on their own. If the org picked B, or picked A but caved on the date under pressure from the same three accounts once it approached, the double-maintenance cost that motivated the memo keeps compounding, and the org is back here in another six months with the same unresolved call and the same missing migration-cost data — a failure of the decision never actually landing, not a customer-facing incident.

assumption.response:
Options and their single load-bearing premise:

- **A — firm 6-month sunset + usage floor + staged notice + hands-on help for the three loud accounts.** Load-bearing dependency: *that per-customer migration cost fits inside a 6-month runway.* The memo says this exact number isn't known. If NorthRoute's parser rewrite/QA, Ferrymark's nightly workflow rebuild, or Tavo's "no bandwidth this year" actually take longer than 6 months, option A flips — the date is too short and becomes de facto option C.

- **B — keep v1 alive indefinitely until complaints stop.** Load-bearing dependency: *the ongoing cost of maintaining two divergent code paths is tolerable.* The memo states every bugfix is already written/tested twice. If that maintenance burden is not tolerable (which the memo's framing suggests it isn't), B flips — indefinite maintenance is the more expensive path, not the safe one. Secondary risk baked into the same premise: complaints may never fully stop, since there's no forcing function to migrate.

- **C — aggressive 60-day sunset on the 0.4% number alone.** Load-bearing dependency: *traffic share is a good proxy for migration cost/impact.* The complaints are direct evidence against this — low volume but each account describes non-trivial rework (response-shape rewrite, zero eng capacity, load-bearing nightly job). If traffic % doesn't track migration cost (which the complaints suggest), C flips — 60 days is too aggressive for at least some of these three.
